answer.summary: Kesavananda Bharati (1973) is before the Supreme Court of India (from Kerala), citation 1973 LawSuit(SC) 155, decided 24 April 1973. The petitions challenged the validity of the Constitution (Twenty‑fourth, Twenty‑fifth and Twenty‑ninth Amendment) Acts; one lead petition (Writ Petition No.135/1970) was filed under Article 32 by Kesavananda Bharati seeking enforcement of fundamental rights (Arts. 25, 26, 14, 19(1)(f) and 31) and challenging Kerala land‑reform provisions. The reported judgment is organized into eight parts addressing (inter alia) interpretation of Golaknath, the original Article 368, and the validity of the specified constitutional amendments. The provided evidence covers only the opening portions of the judgment (paras 1–9) describing issues and structure of the judgment; it is partial and does not include the Court’s final conclusions or holdings. If you want a full summary of holdings and reasoning, I can retrieve or summarize the remaining parts of the judgment.

answer.summary: The basic structure doctrine, as developed by the Supreme Court in Kesavananda Bharati and followed in later decisions, means that Parliament’s constituent power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 is not unlimited: amendments that destroy or alter the Constitution’s essential features (its “basic structure”) can be struck down. The doctrine is not a single textual provision but a set of inferences drawn from the Constitution as a whole; what is said to be a basic limitation must be justifiable by reference to express constitutional provisions and flow naturally from them. Courts have stressed that the doctrine is a tool of constitutional construction to protect the identity of the Constitution (for example the “golden triangle” of Articles 21, 14 and 19) and must be used with care and restraint. It applies to the amending power (not to ordinary legislation) and has been invoked to invalidate amendments or provisions that improperly enhance Parliament’s own power or obliterate fundamental features of the Constitution.

answer.summary: Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati differ mainly on Parliament’s power to amend the Constitution. In Golaknath, the majority held that the power to amend was not located in Article 368, and therefore Parliament could not abridge or take away Fundamental Rights through amendment. Even so, the Court used prospective overruling, so earlier constitutional amendments were left undisturbed. Kesavananda Bharati overruled Golaknath on that point. The evidence says it held that the power to amend the Constitution is found in Article 368 itself, and that this is a constituent, not merely legislative, power. It also treated the 24th Amendment as clarifying that Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights and that Article 13(2) is not a bar to such amendments. The common ground between the two cases is that both were centrally concerned with the scope of Parliament’s amending power and the status of Fundamental Rights; the key difference is that Golaknath denied that Article 368 conferred that amendatory power in the relevant sense, while Kesavananda affirmed it and expressly overruled Golaknath.

answer.summary: The key difference is that Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati took opposite positions on Parliament’s power to amend Fundamental Rights. In Golaknath, the majority held that Parliament had no power to amend Fundamental Rights so as to take them away or abridge them. The reasoning, as later described in Kesavananda, was that Article 368 then laid down only the procedure for amendment, not the substantive power, and that Article 13(2) applied to constitutional amendments. Even so, Golaknath preserved earlier amendments through prospective overruling. Kesavananda Bharati overruled Golaknath on that central point. It held that the power to amend the Constitution is found in Article 368, that this is a constituent power, and that Article 13 is not a bar to constitutional amendment. But Kesavananda did not give Parliament an unlimited power: the evidence shows it recognized implied limits in the form of the Constitution’s “basic structure.” So, the common ground is that both cases dealt with the scope of the amending power and the status of Fundamental Rights; the crucial shift is from Golaknath’s near-bar on abridging Fundamental Rights to Kesavananda’s broader amending power subject to the basic-structure limitation.

messages.content: Compare Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati.

The key difference is that Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati took opposite positions on Parliament’s power to amend Fundamental Rights. In Golaknath, the majority held that Parliament had no power to amend Fundamental Rights so as to take them away or abridge them. The reasoning, as later described in Kesavananda, was that Article 368 then laid down only the procedure for amendment, not the substantive power, and that Article 13(2) applied to constitutional amendments. Even so, Golaknath preserved earlier amendments through prospective overruling. Kesavananda Bharati overruled Golaknath on that central point. It held that the power to amend the Constitution is found in Article 368, that this is a constituent power, and that Article 13 is not a bar to constitutional amendment. But Kesavananda did not give Parliament an unlimited power: the evidence shows it recognized implied limits in the form of the Constitution’s “basic structure.” So, the common ground is that both cases dealt with the scope of the amending power and the status of Fundamental Rights; the crucial shift is from Golaknath’s near-bar on abridging Fundamental Rights to Kesavananda’s broader amending power subject to the basic-structure limitation.

answer.summary: I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu is a Constitution Bench case about the scope of Article 31B and the Ninth Schedule after 24 April 1973, the date of Kesavananda Bharati. From the extract provided, the Court frames the central issue as whether laws inserted into the Ninth Schedule by constitutional amendments after that date continue to get full protection under Article 31B, or whether they can still be tested against the basic structure doctrine. The extract also shows the reference arose in the context of laws that had earlier been struck down, including the Gudalur Janmam Estates Act and a provision of the West Bengal Land Holding Revenue Act, raising the larger constitutional question about using the Ninth Schedule to protect legislation. The practical takeaway from the available material is that placement in the Ninth Schedule, especially after Kesavananda, was being examined as a constitutional question rather than treated as automatically conclusive. The final holding is not fully visible in the supplied evidence.

answer.summary: The core difference is that Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati took opposite views on Parliament’s amending power. As described in the supplied extracts, Golaknath held by a 6:5 majority that the power to amend the Constitution was not located in Article 368, and the effect of that view was that Parliament could not amend Fundamental Rights in a way that abridged or took them away; however, it applied prospective overruling, so earlier amendments were left undisturbed. Kesavananda Bharati overruled Golaknath on that point and held that the power to amend the Constitution is found in Article 368, that it is a constituent power and not merely legislative power, and that the 24th Amendment was largely declaratory of that position. The main common ground is that both cases dealt with the scope of Parliament’s power to amend the Constitution, especially in relation to Fundamental Rights. On the evidence provided, Kesavananda is presented as correcting Golaknath’s view that Article 368 did not contain the amending power. The supplied extracts mainly establish this shift on Article 368; they do not fully set out the broader reasoning beyond that.

answer.summary: The "basic structure" doctrine is a judicially evolved limit on Parliament’s constituent power to amend the Constitution: developed in Kesavananda Bharati and repeatedly explained in later Supreme Court decisions, it holds that amendments under Article 368 cannot destroy the Constitution’s essential features. Its origin and purpose, as described in the cases, were to guard against constitutional collapse and protect fundamental aspects of constitutional governance, democratic form, judicial review and basic human freedoms from being overridden by majorities. The doctrine must be applied with caution: courts require that any claimed “basic feature” be reasonably discernible from the Constitution’s text and structure (it must be correlated to express provisions), and its invocation is meant for protecting core constitutional values rather than as a routine tool for reviewing policy. Subsequent authorities characterise the doctrine as an established axiom that constrains—but does not abolish—the power of amendment, and emphasise restrained, principled use.
